title: 📄 Official Job Description — Fullstack Developer TRAB

sections.heading: Position Details
sections.content:
TitleLSP - Fullstack Developer TRAB (m/f/d)
Location29590 Málaga (Technological Park of Andalusia — PTA)
TypeFull-time
LevelExperienced professionals
Job ID61505
TeamTRAB (Transportabwicklung) — Transport Logistics division
CompanyREWE digital Spain (subsidiary of REWE Group)
Office size70+ colleagues in Málaga
REWE Group~440,000 employees across Germany & Europe
🔗 Original job posting

sections.heading: About the team
sections.content: "Our team of 10 is responsible for developing software solutions that manage distribution to more than 5,700 supermarkets in Germany, supporting warehouse staff, drivers, and logistics planners. In addition, as part of the NEO project, the team is developing a unified communication platform that integrates seamlessly into the REWE digital ecosystem through a modular, plug-and-play architecture."

sections.heading: What you will do
sections.content: • Development and maintenance of web applications, APIs, and backend services for managing transportation and logistics distribution processes. • Implementation of new features and integrations for logistic platforms, connecting internal and external systems. • Creation of interfaces and analytics tools to support warehouses, drivers, and logistics planners in their daily operations. • Collaborating on agile teams to ensure the quality, performance, and availability of business-critical solutions, working alongside stakeholders and multidisciplinary teams.

sections.heading: What you will bring
sections.content:
✅ Strong knowledge and experience in programming with Kotlin/Java
✅ Good knowledge of the current technology stack: Spring Boot, Kubernetes, GitLab, Kafka
🟡 Knowledge of Angular is important, but you don't need to be an expert
💬 Openness and willingness to communicate and work in English
🎯 Ability to work independently and in a goal-oriented manner
🤝 "Team first" mindset — "we see ourselves as a team, not as individuals working in isolation"
🧠 Problem-solving and critical thinking: analyze complex problems, find alternative solutions, make data-driven decisions

sections.heading: What they offer
sections.content: • Startup-like culture + security of REWE Group • Challenging tasks + tech playground • Modern office (creativity, collaboration, agility) • Flexible, autonomous work environment • Open communication in international environment Benefits: • Hybrid work + flexible hours • Private medical insurance (company conditions) • Ticket Restaurant • Professional development (Spanish/English/German courses + IT training) • Day off on birthday • 25 days paid vacation Apply in English with salary expectations.

sections.heading: 🎯 Key signals for interview preparation
sections.content:
What the vacancy tells us about the role:
SignalWhat it means for you
"Development AND maintenance"Hybrid context — not pure greenfield
"Kotlin/Java" (Kotlin first!)Team likely prefers Kotlin for new code
"Angular is important but not expert"You'll touch frontend, but backend is primary
"5,700 supermarkets"Massive scale, operationally critical
"Team first mindset"Collaboration > individual heroics
"Data-driven decisions"Expect metrics, monitoring, evidence-based choices
"International environment"English daily, colleagues in Germany
"NEO — modular, plug-and-play"Architecture questions WILL come up
"Business-critical solutions"Reliability, availability, production readiness matter
"Work independently"Autonomy expected — not hand-holding

title: TRAB Team — What We Know vs What We Infer

sections.heading: ✅ OFFICIALLY CONFIRMED (from vacancy)
sections.content: • TRAB is a product team within REWE Digital's Transport Logistics division • Team size: approximately 10 people • Location: Málaga, Spain (part of REWE Digital) • Tech stack: Java/Kotlin, Spring Boot, Kafka, Kubernetes, Angular, GitLab • Supports distribution to 5,700+ supermarkets • Part of the NEO project (unified communication platform) • Values: critical thinking, data-driven decisions, autonomy, team collaboration Source: Official job posting on LinkedIn and REWE Digital careers page.

sections.heading: 🔶 REASONABLE INFERENCE (from domain + vacancy)
sections.content: • Transportabwicklung translates to Transport Execution / Processing — likely handles the operational phase of deliveries (after planning, before confirmation) • Users probably include: warehouse staff, drivers, logistics planners, store managers • The system probably tracks delivery status across lifecycle stages • Kafka is probably used for event-driven communication between services These are logical deductions from the vacancy and transport domain knowledge. They have NOT been confirmed by REWE.

sections.heading: ❓ QUESTIONS TO VALIDATE IN THE INTERVIEW
sections.content:
• Does TRAB own the entire delivery lifecycle, or only specific phases (e.g., dispatch to confirmation)?
• Who are the primary users? Drivers? Planners? Both?
• Is the Málaga team autonomous in architecture decisions, or coordinated with Germany?
• What's the balance: new features vs modernization vs operations?
• How is real-time tracking handled — does TRAB own it or another team?

sections.heading: 🚫 UNKNOWN — Do NOT claim
sections.content: • Internal architecture details (service boundaries, data models, deployment topology) • Specific business processes or workflows • How NEO works internally • Which parts are legacy vs greenfield • Team velocity, sprint cadence, on-call policies • Revenue impact of transport operations Rule: If the interviewer asks about something in this section, say: "I don't have that information from the public vacancy. Could you help me understand how that works in TRAB?"

title: What Problem is TRAB Probably Solving?

sections.heading: ✅ CONFIRMED signals from the vacancy
sections.content: • The role involves web applications, APIs, and backend services • The domain is transport logistics for 5,700+ supermarkets • Kafka is in the stack → event-driven communication exists • Kubernetes is in the stack → containerized, likely microservices or modular deployment • Angular frontend → planners/operators have dashboards • NEO is a modular, plug-and-play communication platform What we can safely say: TRAB builds software that supports the operational execution of transport logistics at massive scale.

sections.heading: 🔶 REASONABLE INFERENCE — Why they're hiring
sections.content: Probable combination of: 1. Evolution of existing systems — new features, performance, integrations 2. Technological modernization — the stack (Spring Boot, Kafka, K8s) suggests active modernization 3. Scale challenges — 5,700 supermarkets means high volume, reliability requirements I would frame this as a question: "Is the role primarily new development, modernization of existing systems, or a mix?"

sections.heading: 🚫 DO NOT present as fact
sections.content: • Specific delivery workflows (loading → dispatch → tracking → confirmation) — these are domain-generic, not REWE-confirmed • That TRAB handles real-time GPS tracking — another team might own this • That there are "legacy systems" being replaced — the vacancy doesn't say this explicitly • Any internal system names, architectures, or data models Safe interview framing: "Based on the vacancy, I understand TRAB works on transport execution systems at high scale. I'd like to understand: what's the most impactful problem the team is solving right now?"

title: NEO — What We Know and What to Ask

sections.heading: ✅ CONFIRMED (from vacancy)
sections.content: • NEO is mentioned in the job description as a project TRAB participates in • Described as a "unified communication platform" • Described as having "modular architecture" and "plug-and-play" approach That's ALL we know. Three phrases. Everything else is inference.

sections.heading: 🔶 WHAT "unified communication platform" MIGHT mean
sections.content: Possible interpretations (all speculative): • A platform that centralizes communication between internal systems (warehouse ↔ transport ↔ store) • A platform that unifies external carrier integrations • A notification system for drivers, planners, and stores • An event bus / integration layer between microservices We don't know which interpretation is correct. All are plausible.

sections.heading: 🔶 WHAT "plug-and-play" MIGHT mean
sections.content: Possible interpretations: • New carriers/integrations can be added without changing core code (plugin architecture) • New communication channels (SMS, push, email) can be added modularly • New logistics modules can be deployed independently The safest framing for the interview: "I noticed the vacancy mentions NEO as a modular plug-and-play platform. I'd love to understand: what does plug-and-play mean concretely in your architecture — are we talking about deployment modules, integration adapters, or something else entirely?"

sections.heading: 🚫 DO NOT claim
sections.content: • How NEO is implemented internally • That it uses specific patterns (event sourcing, CQRS, hexagonal) • That it has specific services (routing module, template module, etc.) • That you understand its architecture Anything beyond the three confirmed phrases is speculation. Present it as a question, never as knowledge.

sections.heading: Java EE → Spring Boot
sections.content: Transferable (Strong): DI, services, transactions, JPA, REST, validation, testing, business rules Your honest position: "I have 5+ years building enterprise systems with Java EE. The patterns — DI, transactions, persistence, messaging — are identical. I'm learning the Spring Boot API surface, which is the smallest part of the transition."

sections.heading: JMS → Kafka
sections.content: Transferable: async processing, consumers, retries, failure handling, decoupling, eventual consistency New concepts to acknowledge: log-based model, partitions, offsets, replay, consumer groups, retention, ordering by partition Honest position: "I've worked with JMS/Artemis (80+ queues, MDBs, idempotency, DLQ). The messaging PATTERNS transfer directly. What's different in Kafka is the architecture — log-based, consumers track position, replay is possible. I've studied this thoroughly and built practice projects."

sections.heading: WildFly → Kubernetes
sections.content: Transferable: JVM, deployment, resources, production troubleshooting, configuration, monitoring New concepts: pods, probes, replicas, rolling deployments, resource limits, graceful shutdown Honest: "I'm learning K8s concepts. I understand containerization and the operational model, but I haven't managed production clusters."

sections.heading: PrimeFaces → Angular
sections.content: Transferable: component-based thinking, forms, state management, backend integration, validation Honest: "I have extensive UI experience with server-side rendering (JSF/PrimeFaces). Angular is new to me — I understand the concepts (components, services, TypeScript) but I'm not proficient yet. This is a growth area."

sections.heading: PostgreSQL + Performance
sections.content: Direct match (Strong): indexes, EXPLAIN ANALYZE, query optimization, CTEs, partitioning, N+1 diagnosis, connection pool management "This is one of my strongest areas. I've optimized systems with 2000+ tables, diagnosed production issues with connection pools, and built efficient queries for high-volume operations."

sections.heading: What is SinapiPRO?
sections.content:
🔗 github.com/SergioPacheco/sinapiPRO SinapiPRO is an open-source ERP for construction project management — budgeting, measurements, scheduling, procurement, finance, safety, and analytics.
Stack: Java 25 + Spring Boot 4 + Angular 19 + PrimeNG 19 + PostgreSQL 17 + Docker + Kubernetes (Helm)
Why it's relevant to REWE TRAB: This project demonstrates EVERY technology in the job description — built as a production-grade system, not a tutorial.

sections.heading: 🎯 Direct match to TRAB vacancy requirements
sections.content:
TRAB RequirementSinapiPRO Implementation
Java/KotlinJava 25 with Virtual Threads, Structured Concurrency, Records, Sealed Classes
Spring BootSpring Boot 4 — REST, Security, WebSocket, Actuator, Profiles
KubernetesHelm charts, K8s manifests, health probes, graceful shutdown
AngularAngular 19 — Signals, Standalone Components, PrimeNG, i18n, Cypress E2E
CI/CD (GitLab)GitHub Actions (same concepts — pipeline stages, testing, deployment)
Backend services + APIs30+ REST modules with OpenAPI 3.1, ProblemDetail (RFC 9457)
IntegrationsElasticsearch, Keycloak, AWS S3, WebSocket (STOMP), external APIs
Analytics toolsDashboards, ECharts, business metrics, Grafana integration
Quality & performanceTestcontainers, ArchUnit, Resilience4j, Caffeine cache, Virtual Threads
ObservabilityPrometheus + Grafana + OpenTelemetry + Micrometer + custom business metrics
SecurityJWT + OAuth2 + Keycloak + RBAC + Multi-tenant (row-level isolation)
Modular architectureVertical slices per domain (budget, measurement, schedule, procurement...)

sections.heading: Architecture decisions that demonstrate seniority
sections.content: 1. Vertical Slice Architecture: Each module (budget, measurement, schedule) is a self-contained vertical slice with its own api/application/domain layers — exactly like microservices boundaries but inside a modular monolith. 2. Virtual Threads (Project Loom): Enabled for all I/O operations — no need for reactive programming complexity while achieving high concurrency. 3. Structured Concurrency (JEP 480): Used for parallel report generation — multiple data sources queried simultaneously with proper cancellation and error handling. 4. Circuit Breaker (Resilience4j): External integrations (weather API, S3) protected with circuit breakers — system degrades gracefully when dependencies fail. 5. Multi-tenant with Row-Level Isolation: Single deployment serves multiple construction companies — each sees only their data. Same challenge as serving multiple REWE warehouses. 6. Event-driven notifications: WebSocket + STOMP for real-time updates — when a measurement is approved, all stakeholders see it immediately. 7. Testing strategy: • 46 backend unit tests (JUnit 5 + Mockito) • 12 integration tests (Testcontainers — real PostgreSQL) • 38 frontend unit tests (Jest) • 8 E2E tests (Cypress + accessibility checks) • Architecture tests (ArchUnit) 8. Observability from day one: Business metrics (not just technical) — "measurements approved per day", "budgets created" — same approach REWE would need for "deliveries completed per hour."

sections.heading: How to present this in the interview
sections.content: 30-second pitch: "I built an open-source ERP for construction management — SinapiPRO. It uses Java 25, Spring Boot 4, Angular 19, PostgreSQL, Docker, and Kubernetes. It has 30+ business modules, each as a vertical slice. I chose this stack specifically to demonstrate proficiency with modern Java features like Virtual Threads, plus production concerns like observability with Prometheus/Grafana and security with Keycloak." When they ask about Spring Boot experience: "While my production experience is with Java EE, I built SinapiPRO from scratch with Spring Boot 4. I implemented REST APIs with ProblemDetail error handling, @Transactional services, Spring Security with JWT and OAuth2, Spring Data JPA with Testcontainers, and Actuator for health checks. You can see the code on my GitHub." When they ask about Kubernetes: "In SinapiPRO, I created Helm charts for Kubernetes deployment — including health probes, resource limits, secrets management, and horizontal scaling configuration. It's in the /helm directory of the repo." When they ask about Angular: "I built the frontend with Angular 19 using Signals for state management, standalone components, PrimeNG for the UI, i18n with three languages, and Cypress for E2E testing. I'm not an expert, but I can build functional UIs and understand the Angular mental model." When they ask about testing: "SinapiPRO follows the testing pyramid: unit tests with Mockito for business logic, integration tests with Testcontainers for real PostgreSQL queries, ArchUnit for architectural boundaries, and Cypress for critical E2E flows including accessibility checks." Link: github.com/SergioPacheco/sinapiPRO

sections.heading: Parallels between SinapiPRO and TRAB
sections.content:
SinapiPRO (Construction)TRAB (Transport Logistics)
Construction projects with multiple stakeholdersDeliveries with warehouses, drivers, planners
Measurement lifecycle (draft → submitted → approved)Delivery lifecycle (planned → dispatched → delivered)
Multi-tenant (construction companies)Multi-warehouse / multi-region
Real-time notifications (WebSocket)Real-time tracking (driver positions, status)
External integrations (weather API, S3)External integrations (carriers, driver apps)
Analytics dashboards (costs, progress)Analytics dashboards (delivery metrics, delays)
RBAC per module (who can approve what)Permissions per role (planner vs driver vs admin)
PDF/Excel report generationLogistics reports and analytics exports
Vertical slices (budget, measurement, schedule)Modular services (delivery, routing, communication)
Circuit breaker on external APIsResilience for carrier integrations
Key message: "I built the EXACT type of system TRAB develops — operationally critical, multi-stakeholder, event-driven, with real-time updates, external integrations, and production-grade observability. The domain is different (construction vs logistics), but the engineering challenges are identical."

title: Questions to Ask REWE — Demonstrating Senior Thinking

sections.heading: Questions that validate assumptions
sections.content: These show you've researched but don't assume: • "What is the most important production problem you expect the person joining to help solve in the first three months?" • "Why is the position open — team growth, replacement, a new product, or a modernization initiative?" • "Roughly how much of the work is new development vs legacy modernization vs operational maintenance?" • "What does 'plug-and-play' mean concretely in NEO — deployment modules, business capabilities, or external integrations?"

sections.heading: Questions about architecture ownership
sections.content: • "Which architectural decisions are owned by the Málaga team, and which are coordinated with teams in Germany?" • "Where is Kafka currently used, and which delivery/ordering/idempotency guarantees are most important?" • "What is the current balance between Java and Kotlin in the codebase?" • "How is production responsibility organized? Does the team participate in on-call or incident response?"

sections.heading: Questions about failure and operations
sections.content: • "Which failure scenarios currently create the greatest operational impact for warehouse staff, drivers, or planners?" • "How do you measure the team's success beyond completing planned tickets?" • "What would distinguish excellent performance from acceptable performance after six months?" • "What technical assumption about the role do candidates commonly misunderstand?"

sections.heading: Why these questions work
sections.content: They demonstrate: • You've researched the vacancy (specific to TRAB, not generic) • You think about impact, not just tasks • You care about operational reality, not just greenfield design • You're honest about not knowing internals They avoid: • Pretending to know how NEO works internally • Asking questions you could have googled • Generic 'what's the culture like?' questions • Yes/no questions that don't generate conversation
